claimed_contribution: The paper identifies reaction mode as a key boundary condition for cultural influence in persuasion, showing that culture does not have a constant effect on judgments.
It proposes and supports a correction account in which deliberation shifts processing from chronically accessible cultural knowledge to personal knowledge and internal debate.
It offers implications for global marketing communications by indicating that culture-matched value appeals are strongest when audiences process messages quickly or under limited resources.
context: The studies are set in advertising persuasion contexts with English-speaking university students in the United States and Hong Kong. Participants evaluated promotion-focused versus prevention-focused appeals for consumer products (Welch's grape juice and a fictitious sunscreen brand).
design_and_data: The paper reports a pilot and three between-subject experiments using U.S. and Hong Kong samples, plus Asian-American and Anglo-American participants in the pilot. Reaction mode was manipulated through immediate versus deliberative instructions (pilot), an implicit sentence-priming task (Study 1), cognitive load via an 8-digit memory task (Study 2), and perceived time pressure while holding actual ad exposure time constant (Study 3). Key dependent variables were ad attitude ratings and appeal-focus manipulation checks; Study 2 added a pizza-delivery attribution judgment for generalization. Study 3 collected open-ended reasons and coded them as default message-focused thoughts versus personal correction thoughts, then tested mediation.
main_findings: Under immediate, primed-immediacy, cognitive-load, or high-time-pressure conditions, North Americans were more persuaded by promotion-focused appeals and Chinese participants were more persuaded by prevention-focused appeals.
When participants deliberated, had greater cognitive resources, or felt lower time pressure, these culture-based persuasion differences largely disappeared.

Process evidence supported correction: high time pressure effects were mediated by default message-focused thoughts, whereas low time pressure increased personal experience based correction thoughts; a separate attribution task showed a similar culture effect pattern mainly under load.
one_line_summary: Across four experiments, culture-congruent persuasion effects appeared during reflexive processing but attenuated when deliberation enabled correction through personal knowledge.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior cross-cultural persuasion work on promotion versus prevention fit by showing that these cultural preference effects depend on reflexive versus deliberative processing conditions. It also connects dynamic constructivist culture findings with correction models of judgment.
research_gap: Prior studies showed that culture-congruent appeals can be persuasive, but they were less clear about when such effects should emerge versus fade across judgment contexts. The paper addresses the missing account of how processing mode changes reliance on shared cultural norms versus personal experiential knowledge.
research_question: When does culture matter in persuasion judgments? The paper asks whether cultural differences in responses to promotion- versus prevention-focused ads arise mainly under reflexive, automatic responding, and whether deliberative processing corrects these default culture-based judgments by activating personal knowledge.
